summary:
The Canadian government bond market has transitioned into a "bear flattening" phase, with yields surging from their late-February lows (2Y: 2.39%; 5Y: 2.67%) to recent peaks. As of March 25, 2026, the market is grappling with the fallout from a poorly received U.S. 2-year Treasury auction on March 24, which triggered a global sell-off. This U.S. volatility has spilled over into Canada, pushing the 2-year yield to 2.95%—a significant 70-basis-point premium over the Bank of Canada’s 2.25% policy rate. The 2-5 year spread has collapsed from 36 bps in early February to a tight 19 bps, signaling that while the BoC remains on hold, the market is aggressively pricing in persistent inflation and higher global term premiums.

For our institution, this "yield shock" creates an immediate challenge for margin management. The surge in the 5-year yield toward 3.2% necessitates an upward adjustment to our 5-year fixed mortgage rates to maintain profitability against rising wholesale funding costs. Simultaneously, the spike in the 2-year yield to nearly 3.0% exerts intense pressure on our GIC pricing; to remain competitive and retain retail deposits, we must raise short-term deposit rates. However, with the 2-5 spread narrowing so sharply, our lending margins are being compressed—we are essentially forced to pay a higher premium for shorter-term funding while the yield advantage for locking in longer-term assets is evaporating. Management should prioritize agility in retail rate-setting to defend Net Interest Margins (NIM) as the US-Canada yield correlation remains at a fever pitch.
